title: Marbling book edges
subtitle: Applying a marbled pattern to the three exposed edges of a text block
excerpt: Edge marbling, applying a marbled pattern to the fore-edge, head, and tail of a bound or sewn text block, is one of the decorative finishes of the classic trade binding. It is done before the cover is attached, on a compressed and trimmed block. The pattern is applied with the same carrageenan bath used for marbled endpapers.
sourceNotes: Edge marbling technique synthesised from established bookbinding and marbling practice. The method of compressing the block under boards before edge marbling is consistent with historical trade-binding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The open edge of a book opposite the spine. In a standard codex, the fore-edge is the edge you hold to open the pages. The fore-edge is the most visible edge when the book is closed.

A simpler alternative to marbled edges: a fine spatter of diluted ink or colour applied by flicking a loaded brush across a comb held over the block. Less precise than marbling but achieves a similar decorative effect.

content.text: Edge marbling requires the text block to be compressed in a press so the pages cannot fan apart when the edge contacts the marbling bath. If any pages splay outward during the dip, they absorb size and pigment individually rather than as a single unit, and the pattern will be ragged and uneven. The block must also have been trimmed square at all three edges before marbling. A simpler alternative is
, which needs no bath and no mordanting.

attrs.title: Do this before casing in
attrs.body: Edge marbling is done on the sewn text block before the boards and cover are attached. You cannot marble the edges of a finished, case-bound book without damaging the boards. If you are adding marbled edges to an existing bound book, mask the cover boards with tape before touching the tray.

content.text: The exposed paper at the edges needs to be mordanted with alum solution for the pigment to transfer. Dissolve 15 g of potash alum per litre of warm water. Compress the block in the press and, with the
exposed, brush the alum solution onto the edge with a flat brush. Allow to dry completely before compressing again for the marbling step. Repeat for head and tail.

Compress the book block tightly between two boards in the press. The boards should come to within about 5 mm of the edge being marbled, so that edge is fully exposed but the pages cannot fan. Tighten firmly: the block should feel rigid when you press the edge with a finger. A block that flexes under finger pressure will fan during the dip.

Prepare the marbling tray with size and colours as for flat marbling. Lower the exposed edge of the compressed block onto the size surface in a single smooth motion, as if laying a piece of paper. The edge should rest flat on the surface, do not push it below the size. Hold for 10, 15 seconds, then lift away, again in a single motion. Rinse the edge immediately under cool running water. Do not open the press until the edge is dry.

Once the edge is dry (30, 60 minutes, or overnight if using slow-drying pigments), open the press and check that the pages have not stuck together at the edge. If any pages have stuck slightly, separate them carefully with a bone folder. Repeat for the remaining two edges, mordanting and re-pressing for each.
